And Peter used the strategy, Bill. Summers, why his strategy for filling his book of consulting business is, I think, one of the most interesting that I have ever seen, because Summers does not like to market or promote himself, but he likes to be busy, and he likes to do a lot of consulting work. And he does a fair amount of it with speakers. So Summers makes himself a center of relationships between all these other speakers. And what Summers has done now for 15 years is, the night before the National Speakers Association Convention. He used to do it in the same hotel, they won't let him do it there anymore. But actually, what they took away makes it work better, ironically. He holds a big, formal dinner now in another hotel, close to, in walking distance if possible, to the convention hotel. It's by invitation only. There'll probably be, I don't know, 2,000, 2,500 people to convention, and there's probably like 10% of that. There's probably 250, 300 people at Summers' dinner. Anybody been? You have, you have, you have, you have, you have, yeah, you were there last year, weren't you? How'd you get in? So Summers throws this dinner, and he makes a big deal out of getting an invitation and getting a cup. And he sort of strategically picks people that he thinks he can hook together one way or another, thereby creating obligation from both to him, because he's the one who put him together. He strategically picks people who are gonna make him look good by what they say about him. He strategically picks people that he wants to get his clients, but will never overtly suggest to them that they should be clients. And so it's all by invitation. And so now, and this is formal, it's a formal dinner. So ladies, gowns, gentlemen, suits are okay, but tuxedos are suggested. So now the first thing that happens is, this is the night before the industry convention, right? So now, coming out of the elevators and marching through the lobby of the hotel, as everybody else is checking in and arriving or hanging out in the lobby and greeting their friends, is this army of dressed to the nines, members of the association. Where are they going? How come I'm not going? What is this? They're going to Summers' white dinner. And then by the way, about four hours later, they all march back in, of course, while everybody else is hanging out in a cocktail lounge and arriving late and checking in. In comes 250 people in a swarm in tuxedos and long gowns, all yammering about the dinner that they were just at that you didn't get to go to and you didn't get an invitation to. So now everybody's, what went on? They want to know what went on and the thing they weren't a part of, so they're all talking about it. And they all want to get an invitation for next year. How can I get an invitation? Can you get me an invitation? I'm sure you got, everybody who goes gets the, can you get me in? Can you get me in? Can you get me in? Can you get me in? So you really have sort of superseded the association's entire convention with this event. Then at the dinner, he force seats everybody so you're seated where he puts you. He divides couples so you don't get to sit together, which has one guy said to me, you know, I've been trying not to sit next to her for 20 years, it's the greatest thing that happened. So he, I could finally have a dinner in peace. So he divides the couples, they don't get to sit next to each other. And he has very meticulous, he God knows how much time he picks up, he puts on this, but he has put you next to somebody, across from somebody who you are gonna connect with. You're, there's some reason for you to be there that in conversation will surface and you're gonna be able to do business with each other, or you're gonna be able to, you're gonna wanna endorse his book, or you know, so he's really creating without ever calling this, yet sort of a networking, joint venture kind of opportunity. Then he goes around with a microphone prior to the first course and in between courses and stands people up. It appears to people almost to random, but it isn't. He stands people up and he interviews them with like, real quick, two or three questions. None of that is, he's talked to every single one of these people, he's figured out what he's gonna ask them. He's sort of almost fed them what he wants them to say, and he is standing up everybody, giving them their little moment in the sun and having them talk in a way that makes him, in part, sound like a jam. And of course, everybody's very gracious, because why wouldn't you be gracious, right? You're like obligated to be gracious. So without a lot of prompting, everybody gives their answers, but also says something nice about summers. So it's about probably 10% of the entire time is like a giant infomercial for summers white, that nobody really takes it as that. And then halfway through the dinner, there's numbers under the plates and everybody, what, the women stay put and the guys move, right? Yeah, so now all the guys gotta get up and move and it reshuffles where everybody seated and everybody sits back down, now you meet four new people, right? Which the second thing was done strategically and deliberately just like the first group. So now everybody has connected with eight other people during the night and you've had an opportunity to talk to each other, plus he stood everybody up and kind of showcased who they are and what they do and what they're all about. So at the end now, everybody's trying to find, this guy's trying to get over here to talk to this one and this one's getting over here and talking to this one and so forth. And then at the end, then they all march back to the hotel and create this giant to-do when they all are getting out of cabs and walk it in and they're all dressed to the nines and everybody sees it and everybody wants to be a part. He books his whole year of business this way. This is it, it's all he does. There's never, by the way, never a pitch. There's never a strong takeaway, but there's never a big pitch either. But it's very clear, obviously, by the end of the evening, that he does consulting, he does private consulting, he accepts clients for long-term private coaching relationships, and he speaks and for this person, he's done this, for this person, he's done that, for this person, he's done this. But it's very smoothly, elegantly done. It's the whole marketing because two things happen. A bunch of immediate business comes out of it. People now come to them or call them up, but I was at the dinner and, you know, take me. I heard what you did for this person, I heard what you did for take me.
